comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

The image?  That's the seventh card in The Sacred Geometry Oracle. Card 7, The Equilateral Triangle, when upright tells you that you or the situation will benefit from taking things in the direction of more form and structure; when reversed, it tells you that you or the situation have gone overboard with form and structure, and they're now restricting what you can get down. The oak leaves on the sides of the image tell you that this card belongs to the first third of the oracle, which corresponds to Calas, the principle of solidity and materiality.

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

comments.title: Numerology and Ruling Deities
comments.content:

2. Also, since I've decided to use numerology as my daily divination system, would it also be a good idea to make an offering to the Deity that will be ruling over that day for me?

replies.title: Re: Numerology and Ruling Deities
replies.content: Those are certainly options. Have you tried some form of divination to check?

replies.title: Re: Numerology and Ruling Deities
replies.content: I've been learning Tarot, so I'll do that.  Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Numerology and Ruling Deities
replies.content:
Okay, so, I asked two question, which were basically the two questions that I asked you.

For the first question, about Mercury, I drew a reversed Wheel of Fortune. I've decided to make this year the year that I finally make major changes in my life: break free of the wage slave jobs that I've been stuck with and pursue some kind of independent, non-traditional employment, to finally take up the serious practice of magic, etc. So, I interpreted this as meaning "yes, I should make regular offerings to Mercury." I got the sense that, with hard work and dedication, he'll help me reverse my fortunes and lead a more productive, fulfilling life.

For question 2, I got (after shuffling the cards again), the Wheel of Fortune, again, though it was not reversed. I took this as a favorable answer, too, and that this practice of offering devotion to each of the Deities will contribute to my success.

Of course, I'm very new to Tarot, and concerned that I've misinterpreted or missed something crucial here.

comments.title: Firey Spear
comments.content:
From ‘Experience of the Inner Worlds’ page 42.

“The construction of the Fiery Spear in the imagination, and the consciousness of its coming from the All-Highest and penetrating every level of being, passing symbolically through the top of the head above, transfixing the spine and going deep into the heart of the Earth, is an act of will in accordance with the Will of God, and an invocation of the Reality that the symbol represents – the Love and Grace of God to all Creation through Man. It has to be Man in Christ however. The Manhood taken up into Godhead by the Passion of Jesus. The archetype of the Spear that we visualise is the one that is prominent also in the Holy Grail legends – the spear that was suspended over the Holy Grail dripping with three drops of blood ….”

1) I was initially visualising the spear as basically a shaft/cylinder of light (approx. 12 cm in diameter) from God vertically through my body and into the centre of the earth but the discussion of the archetype has me wondering if there is a different image involved. Google was no help to me trying to find what the Grail Spear over the cup looks like. Am I on the right track and/or do you have an image of what he is talking about?

2) The reference to ‘penetrating every level of being’ … would that be helped by visualising it going through each of the Chakras in turn on the way through you (and as I learn more about those adding meaning to the process I imagine)? (update – when I tried imagining a shaft of light and deliberately inviting/opening each point to its progression within me and finally once at the feet to pass through me into the earth I could feel a progressive sensation (kind of tingling) filling each part of me as I opened each part of the channel and once I let the flow out the other end (mentally completing the circuit) the tingling was left mainly in my arms and a sense of peace and joy inside – once I left the ‘session’ and turned back to the task at hand I nearly cried, then laughed, then was left with what felt like a heightened buzzing sensation in my whole body with an emphasis on the chest/shoulder area and the sacral … well that was my experience on Sun morning – not sure if real or psychosomatic but certainly interesting))

3) The first exercise (Sphere of Light) the book says to do morning and evening for 10 mins (then vary according to individual), and, once practiced, anytime of the day where you encounter stress. Later it says “They are, like all the exercises in this book, to be used in conjunction one with another – and in the order given” … assuming this means that you do both whenever you do the first so the referenced time of stress would be included … I am thinking that anytime you are aligning your will with His will that you should also be strongly aware and open to His working through you (you being a priest to His creation). When you finish the time ‘waiting on God’ (Sphere) and ‘dedicating yourself to His will’ (spear) do you just move on or mentally close the images (it is stated that these elements you are imagining cause an objective reality on other planes (in the inner worlds), or pause for say a heartfelt Amen and turn to whatever is before you to do or…?

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content:
1) We don't know what the original Grail spear looked like. Any image you find appealing will be suitable.

2) I don't recommend messing with the chakras unless you're under the close supervision of someone experienced in handling the results. You can mess up your endocrine system and die. Yes, I mean that literally. As a rule, don't add anything to an exercise unless you know for a fact the teacher wanted you to put it in.

3) I'd recommend just moving on.

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content:
1) So a rod of force running from above through your head, down your spine and out between your feet into the earth like a continuous steam of light is fine (what I have been using) or does it need to have a discrete start and finish where it passes through you to match up with later symbolic aspects?

2) Well there is a good lesson. I thought of the Chakras because I had spotted some diagrams in the next chapter one of which had a body outline with them showing. When I read the chapter this evening that was just one of the various (about 9) ways used to illustrate various aspects of the inter-relationship between the physical, inner and spiritual worlds - and no where in it did it talk about using them in what we are doing. The next exercise (once the first two are embedded as a necessary perquisite) also involves the spine (Build a Rod of Power that is formed by a flame of fire rising from the base of the spine to fill the cranium with flame. There must be the realisation and intention that this is a dedication of the totality of all our human powers and faculties to the service of Almighty God.) but none of that involves using chakras. So as you said just do exactly as instructed ... and don't assume/extrapolate or guess and read carefully. After all if you don't trust your guide to know what he is doing why are you following his instruction?



In light of that next exercise - would you need to stop the downward flow of the spear before engaging in the upward flow of the Rod or are they fine to combine?

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's fine.

2) Yes, exactly. Follow the instructions to the letter and you'll be fine.

3) Well, what does Knight say about that?

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content:
3) For the Sphere we get to do 10 mins morning and evening but vary to taste and once you have it down do anytime you are in a stress situation.

For the Spear it doesn't specify except that it says 'They are, like all the exercises in this book, to be used in conjunction one with another – and in the order given' and there are no instructions re when or how to stop.

For the Rod no timing references either - it is just the exercise as I wrote above. There are some warnings about anticipated initial side effects and making sure you have the first two in place properly before going on and how the lack of specific postures and breathing stops it being as dangerous as the version in Kundalini Yoga.

So basically I have do this and this and get those right then add that - if he does not specify how they interact am I to assume that I do the oft-mentioned Greer option of try each combination and see what works for you? (eg sphere on, flowing spear on, make Rod ... sphere on, flowing spear off ... sphere on spear passed through etc. I am assuming (bad Dreamer) that it is best to do all of these while keeping the Sphere on btw...

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content: You certainly keep the Sphere going the whole time. When I explored the exercises, I found it best to let each of the other sets of imagery go when I moved on to the next. Knight is right about kundalini yoga, though he left out a detail -- the bandhas, specific "locks" or muscle contractions, are crucial for generating the full-blown kundalini effect, and he carefully avoids giving those, too.

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content:
Thank you!

In terms of how long to hold each imagination for - my initial thought (inspired by the recipe discussion on the other blog) was 'until it feels right' but that is very subjective and you have spoken to other posters about the dry places we go through on the journey ... is there a standard time people normally use or technique for how to judge that sort of thing or just do it for long enough to show respect for the process or something like that?


Btw is there a real Sphere / Spear etc being created around/through my Astral body in the process of doing this?

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content:
1) There isn't really a standard approach, other than "do it as long as it feels right." Yes, there will be dry patches; do your best.

2) On the astral plane, yes. You're establishing patterns of energy flow and structure that have definite, enduring effects.

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content: 2) Do you have a post talking about that subject ... or is that one of those that would require a book not a post?

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content: I haven't written a post about it, but it's potentially a subject for one. The very, very short form is that our astral bodies are still relatively shapeless and unformed -- we bring form to our bodies starting on the material plane and working up from there, so our material bodies are perfectly formed, our etheric bodies relatively well formed, and our astral bodies pretty vague as yet. Doing work of the kind Knight sketches out -- or the equivalent methods in other systems -- helps develop the astral body by giving it structure and channeling the energy flows in it.

replies.title: Re: Firey Spear
replies.content:
Thank you for your patience today - very much appreciated.

I look forward to seeing the outcome from helping my couch potato astral body turn off the TV, bin the crisps and start to slowly get more active (am guessing that the more in line with each other our material, etheric and astral bodies are the better the whole system works)

replies.title: Re: Speaking in tongues
replies.content:
The anthropologist Tanya Luhrman (yes _Persuasions of the Witch's Craft_) has other books about religious communities. _The Good Parsi_; _When God Talks Back_; and _How God Becomes Real_. I have read Persuasions and When God Talks Back. Her basic theme is a study of how people become part of a non-mainstream faith or practice. I don't always agree with her conclusions, but the study is worthwhile. She also wrote a book on how Freudian psychologists are trained in that viewpoint, _Of Two Minds_. She teaches at Stanford University and is currently studying the effect of homelessness on schizophrenia.

Luhrman created some controversy in the Pagan community after Persuasions was published. Some felt that her immersive research led her subjects to forget they were being studied and to feel that she was becoming part of the community. It isn't clear, or I don't recall, whether she accepted some level of Wiccan initiation or not. Of course, for an anthropologist to be perceived by colleagues as having 'gone native' is fatal to one's career, so the balance to maintain is rather narrow.

Rita

Church Slavonic was one of the few vehicles that delivered the Tradition of the Ancients to the modern times in a secret fashion ;)

+Love and Light
-AWH

comments.title: The Manly One in Japan
comments.content:
A couple of months back, somebody on one of your blogs posted a YouTube link to a lecture by Manly P. Hall. It turns out that YouTube is host to a treasure trove of his weekly lectures, on a wide variety of topics, and I'm finding them very filling. I am very interested in learning more about this history, but the only specific reference I can find to anything about it online (though I did find more general descriptions of Hall's knowledge of Buddhism) is from your prior Dreamwidth post. replies.title: Re: The Manly One in Japan
replies.content:
I like his books, thanks for letting me know about his lectures!

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: The Manly One in Japan
replies.content: I don't have specific documentary proof that Hall received some form of Shingon ordination. What I know is (a) his book on mandalas includes what was until recently the best discussion of the Taizokai and Kongokai mandalas, including details that as far as I know were not available in any Western language source, (b) he had, and apparently used, a Kokuzo mandala, one of the classic minor Shingon mandalas, (c) his tarot deck is set up to use with a kind of meditation I've only seen discussed in books on Shingon, and (d) he certainly visited Koyasan in Japan, and stayed there for a while -- one of his pamphlets discusses his trip there. As for sources, his book on mandalas and his pamphlet on Koyasan might be places to start.

replies.title: Re: The Manly One in Japan

replies.title: Re: The Manly One in Japan
replies.content: It's discussed in Taiko Yamasaki's book Shingon: Japanese Esoteric Buddhism, if that's any help.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Does anyone have any theories on why it seems like TDS and the less sane versions of wokeness spread most rapidly and easily through the same social circles which went the deepest into the 2012 apocalyptic madness?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
They're all the same thing. The 2012 craze was a gigantic wail of protest on the part of privileged people facing a head-on crash between their overdeveloped sense of entitlement and a universe serenely uninterested in giving them the utopian future of their dreams. The hysterical end of the reaction to Trump was more of the same thing -- how dare those deplorables vote for their own interests rather than ours? The obsessions around wokeness, finally, are frantic attempts to prove that utopia really is within reach, combined with equally frantic competition for deck chairs on the Titanic. Each of these things amounts to a scream of rage at a universe that is failing to provide the overly entitled what they think they're owed.

Reflection, self-knowledge, and a recognition of the reality of limits is a lot saner, but to get there you have to recognize that the universe does not notice that you exist and will not miss you when you're gone. If you've been told all your life that the universe exists to cater to your ego, that's a very difficult recognition to accept.

replies.title: (no subject)

Yo! Universe! Please take note that I exist. Thank you.

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nah, it's a very human sort of craziness. Infants naturally see themselves as the center of the cosmos; with maturity comes a recognition of just now little and temporary a phenomenon each of us really is. There are always those who don't grow up, however, and cling to the infantile fantasy of their own cosmic importance long after it should have been set aside with rattles and diaper pins.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Oh, wokeism is easy to understand if you realise it's a degenerate mystery religion with its own sacred symbols, doctrine and vocabulary, its sacred writings written in a language that nobody can understand, and its self-appointed high priests. Even "woke" is, at a pinch, a religious term, because it equates to "enlightened", or to one translation of the word Buddha - "the man who woke up". Initiates claim to see the world as it really is, and they have ultimately a platonic conception of reality, that ideas are more "real" than mundane things. Thus, purely verbal activities (changes to pronouns or deletion ofwords) are held to affect reality itself, thus giving them power over a universe that appears meaningless. Most of all, wokeism is full of sects that hate each other, yet differ only mildly on points of doctrine: Freud's narcissism of minor differences. As a consequence, the full force of woke hysteria is not directed at the Right (which has its own mystery cults) but either at the traditional Left (people like Freddie de Boer) or even worse at heretics and apostates like Rhyd Widermuth. Wokeism is a doctrine for those who want to be religious but have forgotten how.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

2) It's certainly true that some people get better results with certain oracles than with others. Beyond that, as far as I know, the experiments haven't yet been done.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
2) This theory is correct. Both the subjective quality of the intuition and the general focus of intuitive insight seems to change based on the divinatory system you are studying. From my experience:

The reason I dislike the tarot is because it has a somewhat dream-like or foggy quality to the intuition it develops. A Russian martial artist of my acquaintance actually prohibits his students from studying tarot for this reason.

Runes seem to come with a very strong sense of weight and balance. Things stand out because they are "heavy" with meaning, probable phenomena have a lot of "weight" to them. My grappling improved dramatically when I took them up as I had a really strong sense of where people's weight was going.

I put a decent amount of time into trying to learn JMG's original sacred geometry oracle. I could never get it to work for actual divination, but I did find a really sharp increase in my ability to detect subtle body alignments and how objects were situated relative to one another. Negative energy also seemed to take on a crooked quality during this time.

Geomancy has a hard and somewhat formal feeling to it. Intuition seems to highlight how events are reflective of more archetypal states. I have a really strong kinesthetic imagination; that may be why this is such a comfortable and effective method for me.

Since taking up astrology (cheery green novice), I've caught myself noticing the passage of time much more frequently.

3) I have two data points that make me think this is a viable workaround:

Tarot only seems to give me an enthusiastic yes to any romance questions. I didn't know that when I asked about a certain girl; and was essentially told we would be getting married. I vividly remember the three card draw I drew the morning of the day we broke up: Lovers, Tower, 10 of Swords.

The runes seem to give me an affirmative whenever I ask a question about something being possible or viable. You can, after all, do anything you want; just not with good results. Working around that limitation was the key for me to get good results. "Should I..." rather than "Can I..." as well as "What forces would affect..." rather than "Is it possible to..."

Excellent questions as always, hopefully this is of use to you.

replies.title: different oracles
replies.content:
The divination tools I have the most and longest experience with are the Tarot (primarily the Waite-Smith deck and the Celtic Cross spread) and the I Ching in the Wilhelm-Baynes translation (using coins to generate the hexagrams). I use minimal ceremony or induction before readings and am only modestly intuitive.

My Tarot readings readily give information about people's motivations and it's easy to get descriptions about the present influences in the querent's life and whether they are passing away or arriving.

When I read cards, my idea of what I am doing is using the visual symbols to bring my subconscious knowledge into consciousness and amplify my intuition. I don't regard the Tarot as a way to access an intelligence outside myself.

The I Ching in its present form has a heavy Confucian influence. I seem to remember that the introductory material in the translation I use suggests that one is consulting a being not on the physical plane, but not a god. So this is an oracle, but not in the Greek sense. When I was a young woman, I found that it refused to give me advice about my love life. I thought about that and decided to imagine the oracle as a consultation with an elderly gentleman retired from a long career in the Imperial bureaucracy. It's no use asking questions about sex and romance; he doesn't care to hear that from me and the only advice he would give was to get married and do what my husband and mother-in-law tell me.

When I take out the book and the coins, my questions are usually about how to deal with bureaucracies and operate within organizations I belong to. I often need advice on when to push, when to use an indirect approach, and when to wait for events to work themselves out. This oracle is very wise and gives clear advice about such questions. I have not seriously studied the symbolic system that underlies the commentaries, but familiarity with the hexagrams has made me more aware about the ebb and flow of forces and how things can only go in one direction so far before they are weakened and have to retreat. I remember this when watching politics.

re 1) I've been making my way through "The Dawn of Everything" by Graeber and Wengrow. The whole book is fascinating, and if you're like me you'll see a lot of connections to JMG's deep historical themes. When they talk about the deep history of Native Americans, I found myself screaming "TAMANOUS!!! THAT'S TAMANOUS!!!" in my head.

Read it if you have the time / interest. I highly recommend. But, you could just skip to the second to last chapter "Full Circle," and dig into its bibliography.

Native American traditions out of Eastern North America tell of rejecting horrible abuses of power in their past, and consciously choosing social arrangements that prevent these abuses from ever happening. This maps onto the historic records: gigantic mounds are sprinkled all over the Mississippi River basin, centered on Cahokia, in an area that by the time Europeans got there was known as "the empty quarter."

comments.title: John Dee
comments.content: JMG - What do you think of John Dee and Enochian Magic?

replies.title: Re: John Dee
replies.content:
The very short form? Dee was an interesting Renaissance magician, and a top-notch astrologer; Elizabeth I asked him to choose a favorable time for her coronation, and you'll notice how that turned out! He was a far more important figure than today's historians like to mention -- he's the guy who invented the concept of the British Empire and played a crucial role in getting the government and the upper classes to support the idea of founding colonies in North America, which is why we're not having this conversation in French or Dutch. (Or Swedish. Did you know that Sweden had a colony in what's now Delaware and western Pennsylvania?)

As for Enochian magic, there are two things that go under that name. The first is the system that Dee himself practiced, using the Holy Table, the Sigillum Dei Aemeth, and shewstones. The second is the version of the system that was reworked over two centuries or so in the British occult scene, and finally got published in the Golden Dawn material. In either form, it's difficult, intense, and potentially dangerous if you don't have ample experience and training, but it can be useful stuff. I did it, of course, as part of my Golden Dawn work back in the day, but I left it out of my books on Golden Dawn magic, because it's not for beginners. I recommend learning the rest of the Golden Dawn system first, and then tackling the Enochian work.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends very, very, very much on which version of Freemasonry we're talking about. French Freemasonry, which I'm guessing is what you're talking about, is considered irregular and invalid by regular Masons. (One of the ancient landmarks of Freemasonry reads as follows: "That every Mason must believe in the existence of God as the Grand Architect of the Universe." Lodges that don't abide by the ancient landmarks are not recognized by regular Masons or their lodges.) It doesn't surprise me that irregular lodges in the French tradition splash Lucifer around their artwork, but you won't find that in any regular lodge!

It's been fashionable for a long time for rationalists of a certain sort to take up a Luciferian pose. mostly as a way to thumb their noses at Christianity -- the Satanic Temple, an outfit here in the US made up of atheists who play at devil worship, is a good example. Greetings JMG! I have a question about the afterlife. Now, as a Christian, my vision is the orthodox one of our souls (whatever those are) staying intact in whatever comes after death. In contrast, reincarnation posits the persistence of the individuality, but the dissolution of the personality, so that while ultimately an individual immortality more or less exists, the idea of spending eternity with one's loved ones does not. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First of all, nobody just "hangs around" in the afterlife. We all have a lot of work to do to process the experiences of the life just ended, reconnect with the Divine to the extent that we're able to do so, and prepare for the next life. In eras when there aren't so many human bodies being born, it's quite common for people to spend the equivalent of many lives in the afterlife before returning to incarnation, and even now it's only those who have a lot of unfinished business to take care of who go back into incarnation after only a few years. Family members and good friends who have gone out of incarnation before you are thus very often available to greet you and help you cope with the shock of transition.

Second, er, were you under the impression that every time you reincarnate, you meet a completely different set of people? Not so. It's very common for a loose network of souls to pass through incarnation more or less together, changing roles from life to life -- your mother in this life may be your son or your best friend the next time around, and so on. Your personality, as the Latin root of that word indicates, is merely a mask over the individuality, a set of habits of thought and feeling you take on temporarily during an incarnation, and generally change quite a bit between birth and death. Your individuality is who you are, and indeed it's who you've been all along.

Oh, and when you feel that you make a deep connection with someone, something that goes deeper than the surface personality? That's your individualities communicating. Again, the personality is just a temporary mask. Love is very often a matter between two individualities, and thus it can endure not only from one incarnation to the next, but beyond the point at which incarnation is outgrown. So you're quite mistaken to say that the concept of spending eternity with your loved ones is absent from the occult view of reincarnation.

replies.title: Re: Disposal of a Malevolent Item
replies.content: First, if you know a banishing ritual, trace a banishing symbol over it and vibrate an appropriate name of power, if possible before touching it. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If it is any help, I thought I'd share some of my own experience looking for a therapist, and I also started out with a preference for a Jungian, as I have found Jung's writings and thinking extremely helpful and insightful.

What I found when I started looking into it, though, is that the Jungian approach is not very popular (to put it mildly) in the academic world of psychology today, which means that the vast majority of licensed therapists have little exposure to Jung except as a historical curiosity (on the way to the more modern, scientific, and of course, "better" psychological approaches today). That means that you're dealing with a much smaller pool of possible therapists, which makes it hard to find a therapist you can work well with, since it's such a personal interaction.

That being said, here are some general guidelines I found helpful in my process:
1) If you can get a recommendation from a friend who has worked with the therapist, that's great. Even if that particular therapist isn't taking clients, they can refer you to others, and there's at least some link to someone you know and trust to be reasonable.
2) If you're in America, historically, being a member of the American Psychological Association has been a pretty good indicator of being upstanding, but in more recent years that organization has signed onto a lot of the political ideology of the times, and it's unclear to what degree that has affected normal membership (or not)
3) Most of the time, it's a pretty good idea to find someone who is at least as smart as you are - you can kinda-sorta use level of education as a proxy for this, but obviously it's not perfect

Whether or not you find the above heuristics helpful, you should talk to any therapist you're considering to get a feel for whether they're a good fit. Most will offer a free consultation to figure that out, and it might be helpful to talk to a handful before making a final decision.

Good luck and I hope you find someone who's able to help you out the way you need!

No. The chakras are specifically spinal centers, while the centers of the Middle Pillar and the Three Cauldrons are along the midline of the body, not along the spine.

Geomancy question. For a house chart, the 2nd house is linked to questions on "profit and loss, income, investments, personal belongings..." whereas the 5th house is, in part, linked to "speculative investments". What's the difference between speculative investments and regular investments?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Easy. An investment is speculative if your hope of making money off it depends on being able to sell it for more than you paid to buy it. If you buy a stock because it pays a good dividend, that's an investment; if you buy a stock because you think you can sell it for more than it cost to buy, that's a speculation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A teacher taught me an interesting technique that I believe could help with your first question.
Most of us are great at envisioning acceptable and disappointing outcomes; but are more prone to embellishing the bad. Rather than "I get the job" or "I don't get the job," the negative would be "they hate me" or "I'm completely under qualified for the job." Both of the more extreme negations play on personal doubts rather than just being the opposite of the desired outcome. Whenever those thoughts come up, balance them with an equally strong opposing positive thought, like "they love me and hire me" or "I'm overqualified for the job and they offer me a better one."
It's fun to eventually play with taking these to further extremes, i.e. "they won't hire me, and I'll lose my current job for applying, and no one will love poor me" or "they will hire me, I quickly become a partner, and I meet the love of my life there." Once you play with how wide the actual realm of possibility is, I find that the negative and positive thoughts will become just that, thoughts; and you can return to your finely-wrought intentions and affirmations, perhaps something like "I am a skilled craftsperson in my field and find well-paying work easily."

Secrecy protects the magic from others' will working in the same space (a parent or friend with their own purposes, a person who covets your time or energy, or an antagonist working against you.) replies.title: Re: Ellipse

First, some background:
I often practice a Q/A style journaling, where I’m having a conversation with...I don’t know. Myself? But “I” (the other voice) seem to know things that I (the person holding the pen) didn’t know.
In asking questions trying to work through some of my deeper fears and anxieties, I’ve traced them through two past lives. I am currently a human (obviously) and female.

Previous to that, human and male, in a time period I’d estimate to be around the late 1800s. And in that life I killed someone innocent and hid the crime and spent a lifetime looking over my shoulder, afraid I’d be found out. This the source of my constant feelings of fear and guilt in this lifetime.

Before that, I was a big cat (snow leopard) and female, estimating early 1700s based on the guns with which the villagers shot me (my memory is of dying, rising up above my body, seeing it laying in the bloodied snow with the villagers standing in a ring around me, watching me die) they thought I had killed one of their children and only realized after they shot me that the child was alive and unharmed. so in that life I was the innocent who died. i have been unable to trace anything before the snow leopard.

Does this sort of alternating fate happen? In one life the victim, in the next the murderer, in the next, still dealing with the consequences of the murder? And why can’t I go farther back than the snow leopard?

Those are the only two lives I remember and I’m quite sure this is only my 2nd attempt at human (I’m not doing it very well)

Now it gets really strange:
When I first interviewed for my current job, it was not at all like an interview. It was like two old friends sitting down for a chat. I’ve been working for this man for 4 years now and it is eerie how often we read each others minds (full disclosure: I admit having had a crush on him first couple years, but he is very happily married, and I have had enough sense to keep my feelings to myself). Other people have noted how well we work together, handing each other tools without being asked, knowing what needs to be done without the other speaking - even at the beginning when I’d only been working there a few months. So one day as I was journalling, I asked what the connection was all about.

The answer that came back was that we were brothers in a previous life.

This confused me, as I thought it was pretty well established that I had only been human once before, and I didn’t have a brother.

Correct, it said. This particular past hasn’t happened yet.

So. A thing in my past that hasn’t happened yet. I got a brief glimpse of a timeline - a timeline that is non-linear, it loops and folds over itself. Deja-vu happens when two lines get close enough for an effect like tunnelling (see evanescent waves in fiber optics, for example). I asked for explanation, and the answer was: “you know how in your calculus classes sometimes they’d say ‘the proof of this theorem is beyond the scope of this course’? You’re not there yet.”

Are there any teachings out there about time behaving in this manner? Or is it all a bunch of nonsense my brain invented on its own?

replies.title: Re: Past life that hasn’t happened yet
replies.content:
1) Not only does that sort of alternating fate happen, it's very, very common. All of us have been the murderer and the victim, the rescuer and the rescued, and so on through the whole gamut of possible actions. It happens in animal and human lives alike.

2) I've heard some people talk about that. It seems very odd to me, since my past life memories are all along a linear timeline, but apparently it does happen in some situations.

How do you know when a situation you seem stuck with is something you deserve,need to learn from and should accept or if it's something you should be fighting tooth and nail to get out of? Six months after my wife decided to dump me more or less overnight, we are still living together. I tried to deal with things in an enlightened way, knowing that she is dealing with some very difficult inner challenges, but things have ground on and I am simply sick of her, to be blunt. She said that she would basically find a job then find somewhere to live and go, but this doesn't seem to be happening in the near future. The feasibility of that hinges on her having a driver's licence, which she is ready to get but can't do the driving test because COVID restrictions have completely screwed up the system and there is a huge backlog. So, not her fault. She did get the job, which means I am stuck as the primary carer of our son. I haven't had the clot shots and have abandoned the regular workforce and chosen a work from home contract editing job that is very flexible and would bring in decent money if I wasn't stuck with looking after my son. He is notionally in day care four days a week but COVID cases at the day care, minor illness, public holidays etc. mean that he rarely does the four days. There is no end to this situation in sight and it could be months and months before my wife gets out of here and potentially takes him for half the time. I am often extremely frustrated, as I try to get work done and am constantly interrupted by him at times. I am bitter and angry at my life becoming a prison, and have recently been able to accept that I hate my wife for putting us in this situation and loathe her for abandoning most adult and parenting responsibilities.

To refocus, I have done my best accepting all this and trying to remain patient and be a good father, but my ongoing suffering is just creating a bad environment for my son. I aspired to manage this in a way that I thought would be for the highest good of us all, but I am regularly hitting the limits of what I can take and creating astral pollution. I absolutely hate my life and the lack of control I have. I've tried to be 'good' but it hasn't had good consequences and seems to be perpetuating a system where I am creating problems because I have too much suffering to deal with and it is overflowing.

I am continually pushing my son away because I am trying to get things done or I just can't deal with the demands for attention. I have no friends and can't even seek out new ones. Most organized social groups and activities aren't running because Omigosh is running rampant here. I feel completely trapped.

Interpersonal stuff aside, does it seem reasonable from a spiritual and magical perspective to continue to try and accept this state of affairs and grow from it, or do everything I can to change it? Good evening, JMG- I've got yet another neurotic "is this normal/am I doing it right" question for you this week, I'm afraid. I've noticed that when attempting Pathworkings, the experience I have while sitting by myself, quietly in the dark, is anything but vivid, and often feels kind of forced, like I'm fumbling around for images/ideas or "making things up"- often it feels more like I'm trying to brainstorm ideas for a story than anything else (as an example, a recent attempt at the path of Qoph, moving dully through dim water, and I found myself wondering- "what if a giant cuttlefish appeared"- the wondering distinctly preceding anything like an image, and the image remaining fairly abstract/conceptual). I find, however, that the richness of the experience is greatly enhanced by writing it down immediately afterward- oftentimes, things that felt dim, vague and forced in the moment, will seem more vivid and compelling in memory when I put them in narrative form. FWIW, I've always had a much more verbal than visual imagination (bordering on aphantasia), but I'm wondering, I suppose, whether this is a "normal" way to experience pathworking (telling myself a story, as it were, with some self-consciousness about this fact), or if it would be better to be more patient and wait for things to arise more spontaneously, while accepting the fact that this will involve a lot more waiting and "dead air"?

Many thanks, as always!
-NPM

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In fact, that's very common. Since I bet you don't often think of giant cuttlefishes showing up, for that matter, I'd encourage you to consider the possibility that you sensed the giant cuttlefish conceptually before you saw it.

Jungian therapists, when a patient doesn't remember any dreams, sometimes ask them to make up a dream -- and the made-up dream turns out to reveal just as much as an actual sleeping dream. Pathworkings are the same way.
